step_by_step_thinking:
All of the listed fractures mentioned are indicative of certain forms of inflicted injury, whether related specifically to domestic violence or child abuse. However, wormian bones do not represent a fracture nor are they a typical sign of inflicted injury. Instead, wormian bones refer to small irregular ossicles within the lambdoid suture of the cranium and are usually benign variations rather than signs of abuse. Thus, the correct option would exclude something that is unrelated to fractures characteristic of abuse.
\boxed{A} Based on the evidence presented, wormian bones, which are small ossicle formations within the skull, do not represent fractures or typical indicators of abuse-related injuries. The other options—microfractures in the sub-periosteal region, corner fractures (classic metaphyseal lesions), and bucket-handle fractures—are all more closely linked to specific types of inflicted injuries seen in cases of abuse.
